title: 7.2.14 Preemptive Diagonal Tension
content:
7.2.14 Preemptive Diagonal Tension
n this behavior mode, a diagonal tension crack forms
without significant ductile response. Typical diagonal
ension cracking—resulting from strong mortar, weak
nits, and high compressive stress—can be identified
y diagonal cracks (“X” cracks) that propagate through
he units. In many cases, the cracking is sudden and
rittle, and vertical load capacity drops quickly. The
racks may then extend to the toe, and the triangles
bove and below the crack separate. In a few cases, the
oad drop may be more gradual with cracks increasing
n size and extent with each cycle.
A second form of diagonal tension cracking exists with
weak mortar, strong units, and low compressive stress,
when the cracks propagate in a stair-stepped manner in
ead and bed joints. In Specimen MA of Calvi and
Magenes (1994), this behavior was observed (Magenes,
997).
Capacity is based on the FEMA 273 equation for
iagonal tension. This equation requires calculation of
masonry tensile strength, but there is no direct test for
his value. As a substitute, the bed-joint mortar strength
s used. This strength value only applies to the mortar,
ot the masonry units. Thus, there is a great deal of
ncertainty in diagonal tension-strength calculations.
